name: Gamer Sensei
description: Online marketplace for booking one-on-one and team esports coaching sessions from screened pro and semi-pro players, targeting amateur-to-competitive gamers and educational esports programs.

details.employees: Unknown (platform lists ~500 active coaches at times)
details.business: B2C coaching marketplace with B2B partnerships (teams / schools)
details.targetMarket: Casual-to-competitive gamers, parents of youth players, collegiate and high-school esports programs, and amateur teams seeking coaching

items: One-on-one lessons and multi-player/team coaching sessions
Coach search and booking marketplace across ~20+ popular esports titles
Curated coach onboarding and screening process
Coach profiles with bios, past experience and lesson rates
Ability to connect in-game accounts (Steam, Battle.net, Riot) to profiles
Per-lesson / hourly pricing set by coaches (variable pricing)
Scheduling system and notifications (bot / Discord integration)
Subscription / discounted booking options and referral credits (platform offerings)

missingFeatures: No standardized, comparable pricing tiers — coach-set hourly rates vary widely (e.g. $16–$93 reported), making side-by-side value comparison hard for buyers.
Limited measurable progress tracking or structured curriculum per student — platform emphasizes lessons but lacks a visible analytics/progress dashboard to show skill gains over time.
Weak verified outcomes or guarantees — beyond coach bios and ratings there is no consistent, verifiable skill-validation or refund/guarantee framework for high-ticket coaching packages.
Recurring lesson automation is minimal — no clear first-class workflow for recurring weekly lessons, automated practice plans, or curriculum drip for subscription students.
Marketplace quality signaling is coarse — coach quality varies and there is no advanced trust layer (certifications, third-party verification, standardized accreditation) beyond internal screening.

Limited B2B / API offerings for large organizations — while there are partnerships (NACE, team coaching), there is no public API or documented enterprise product for LMS integration or single-sign-on.

pricingPlans.name: Per-lesson (coach-set)
pricingPlans.note: Individual coaches set hourly/session rates; reported student-facing range from low-teens to double-digits higher for pro coaches.

pricingPlans.name: Team Coaching (enterprise / group)
pricingPlans.note: Custom pricing for team / school coaching engagements (launched as a product offering in 2021).

pricingEvidence.label: USA Today (pricing range reported)
pricingEvidence.claim: Reports Gamer Sensei coach prices ranging from about $16/hour for beginner coaches to $93/hour for 'legendary' professional coaches.

pricingEvidence.label: TechieInsider review
pricingEvidence.claim: Notes coach-set pricing starts as low as $5/hour and can go up to hundreds depending on teacher popularity (illustrates variable coach-driven pricing).

companyEvidence.label: CORSAIR press release (acquisition)
companyEvidence.claim: CORSAIR announced acquisition of Gamer Sensei; states Gamer Sensei was founded in 2016 and is positioning it as the world’s biggest esports coaching platform.

companyEvidence.label: Crunchbase company profile
companyEvidence.claim: Company funding history on Crunchbase shows seed and Series A rounds; aggregated funding reported ~$6.3M prior to acquisition.

reviewEvidence.label: Trustpilot review page
reviewEvidence.claim: Customer complaints include coach no-shows and charging issues (example: user reported paying $145 for a lesson where the coach did not show).

reviewEvidence.label: Reddit thread (user complaints)
reviewEvidence.claim: Users report site/checkout problems and duplicate charges, and changes to how coaches are surfaced — signals of trust and UX friction.

featureEvidence.label: How It Works (product features)
featureEvidence.claim: Describes the platform's core features: coach marketplace, one-on-one lessons and built-in scheduling.

featureEvidence.label: Blog: SenseiConnect / feature announcements
featureEvidence.claim: Blog posts describe features like connecting in-game accounts and SenseiConnect (screen sharing / conferencing) to improve lesson delivery.

featureEvidence.label: CORSAIR newsroom (team coaching launch)
featureEvidence.claim: Announces Team Coaching product and built-in video conferencing; confirms platform supports team/school offerings.

strengths: Strong brand and distribution support after acquisition by CORSAIR, which improves trust and reach into hardware customers and esports communities.
Large, curated pool of coaches across many popular esports titles and a screening process that filters coaches before listing.
Product features built for live coaching: built-in conferencing / screen-share (SenseiConnect), scheduling, game-account linking and team-coaching product for schools/teams.
Partnerships with esports organizations and schools (e.g., NACE, HSEL) that open B2B channels and credibility in educational esports programs.
weaknesses: Pricing opacity and high variability: coach-set rates make it hard for buyers to evaluate value; platform lacks standardized packages for easier comparison.
Reported reliability and support issues (coach no-shows, billing glitches) reduce trust for higher-ticket purchases — seen in Trustpilot/Reddit complaints.
Lack of visible, structured progress tracking or measurable outcomes for students, which weakens value proposition for recurring, curriculum-driven customers.
Marketplace quality control is uneven — outcomes depend heavily on the individual coach and there is no widely-adopted external accreditation or standardized curriculum.
Enterprise/technical integration (APIs, LMS connectors) and explicit enterprise product documentation are limited or not public, which raises friction for large customers seeking integration.
